title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, June 16
tldr: Web-dev play of the day: run long AI/agent jobs on Vercel — and now kill them mid-flight. Two changes land back-to-back: Vercel Functions can run up to 30 minutes (1,800s, up from 800s — Jun 15, Fluid Compute, Pro/Enterprise, beta over 800s), and today (Jun 16) the Workflow SDK adds inflight cancellation via the standard AbortController — the cancel signal survives suspensions and replays and crosses step boundaries. Together that's the missing half of durable execution: a report build, a multi-model pipeline, a scrape can now run for half an hour serverless and be aborted the instant the user cancels — so a runaway step can't quietly burn compute. (Cancellation is cooperative: the step must watch the signal or pass it to fetch.)

Email play: stop trusting your ESP's 'delivery rate' — measure inbox PLACEMENT. A 98% delivery rate can hide a ~60% inbox rate: 'delivered' only means the mailbox provider accepted the message, not that it reached the inbox. With Gmail/Yahoo/Microsoft now permanently rejecting (not just spam-foldering) senders who miss the rules — spam-complaint ceiling 0.30%, target under 0.10% — an unmeasured placement gap is lost revenue your client's dashboard says is fine. Run a seed/spam-list test on one live campaign and read folder placement per provider before you send. (Litmus.)

Paid play: Google Ads is recalibrating how budget-limited Smart Bidding hits your targets — re-baseline before Aug 17. Three changes: (1) a backend bidding-target optimization for budget-constrained tCPA/tROAS campaigns (account notices ~Jul 6, rollout starts Aug 17, expect a short calibration wobble); (2) Promotion Mode (beta) to schedule a temporary ROAS-tolerance loosen + extra daily budget around sales spikes; (3) Smart Bidding Exploration now global for Search + feed-less PMax, with a new Shopping beta. Audit your limited-by-budget campaigns and pull stale targets toward real trailing actuals now. (Dates per the announcement — directional until the in-account notice lands.)

AI landscape (news, cross-checked): ChatGPT dropped below 50% assistant share for the first time. Sensor Tower's app-usage data (end-May) puts ChatGPT at 46.4%, Gemini 27.7% (the gainer), Claude 10.3% — note this is app/assistant usage, not web traffic (StatCounter still shows ChatGPT ~80% of chatbot web visits; cite the right metric). Alongside: SpaceX agreed to buy Cursor (Anysphere) for ~$60B, close ~Q3 2026 (Reported; deal structure not primary-confirmed), and the SpaceX IPO total grew to $85.7B after the greenshoe. The read for us: client AI work gets judged in a multi-model world — design for portability, lead with cost-per-outcome.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is add the off-switch, then scale — a 30-minute job needs a cancel signal, a billing-default needs a re-baselined target, a 'delivered' number needs a placement check, a single-model build needs a fallback. The capability is the easy half; the guardrail that stops the silent failure — the abort, the threshold, the seed-test, the second model — is the half that makes it safe to ship to a client.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: run a 30-minute AI/agent job on Vercel and cancel it mid-flight (Functions 30-min + Workflow SDK inflight cancellation)

blocks.sub: Vercel Functions now run up to 30 min (Jun 15) and the Workflow SDK adds standard-AbortController cancellation that survives suspensions/replays (Jun 16) — together, cancelable long-running jobs with no extra infra
blocks.p: What changed (two changelog entries, back to back): (1) Jun 15 — Vercel Functions' max duration rose to 30 minutes (1,800s, up from 800s) for Node.js and Python on Pro/Enterprise, on Fluid Compute (which bills CPU only during active execution and pauses during I/O waits); anything over 800s is in beta. (2) Jun 16 (today) — the Workflow SDK (workflow@beta) added inflight cancellation built on the standard Web AbortController/AbortSignal: the cancel propagates across workflow and step boundaries and stays durable across suspensions and deterministic replay, so a step running as a separate function invocation still sees the abort. Cancellation is cooperative — the step has to inspect the signal (or hand it to a signal-aware API like fetch) to actually stop. Why it matters for our builds: the long client jobs we used to push onto a queue/worker — PDF or video generation, a multi-model AI pipeline, a scrape, a nightly report build — previously hit the 800s wall. Half an hour serverless clears most of them with no infra to stand up. And cancellation is the piece that makes it safe: race a durable timeout against a slow model, cancel the siblings after the first success, or kill the whole pipeline the moment the user navigates away — without leaking compute spend, because Fluid only bills active CPU. It's the same 'never ship an AI feature without a cost ceiling' discipline, now native to the runtime. This is a fresh advance on the durable-execution thread (distinct from the Jun 13 Nitro-v3 integration), not a repeat.
blocks.doIt: (1) On a Pro/Enterprise team, turn on Fluid Compute (Project → Settings → Functions). (2) Install the beta SDK: npm i workflow@beta. (3) Raise the limit on the long route — Next.js App Router: add export const maxDuration = 1800; to the route file; other frameworks: { "functions": { "api/long-task.py": { "maxDuration": 1800 } } } in vercel.json. (4) Make it cancelable — create an AbortController, thread controller.signal into every step/fetch you want killable, and call controller.abort() on the losing branch of a Promise.race (e.g. real work vs a sleep('30s') timeout). (5) Deploy, trigger a run, and confirm the cancel propagates in Dashboard → Observability → Workflows. (6) Put a hard dollar cap in front of any model calls inside the job (card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits) so even a 30-minute window can't run away. Card: card-webdev-vercel-cancelable-jobs.
blocks.note: Honest caveats: both the >800s/30-min tier and the Workflow SDK (incl. cancellation) are beta — pin workflow@beta and expect API churn before GA. Cancellation is cooperative, not preemptive: a CPU-bound loop that never checks the signal won't stop, so pass signal into your awaits. Same-day bonus: the Workflow SDK also added TanStack Start support via the workflow/vite plugin — relevant if a client app is on TanStack. Logged a prediction (the duration tier + cancellation reach GA by ~Sep 16). Sources: Vercel Changelog (×2), Vercel Docs.

sections.h: Email — run it today: audit inbox PLACEMENT, not 'delivery rate' (the metric that's quietly costing your client revenue)

blocks.sub: A 98% delivery rate can sit on a ~60% inbox-placement rate — and now that Gmail/Yahoo/Microsoft permanently reject rule-breakers, the gap is lost revenue, not a spam-folder slap. Measure it with a seed/spam test before you send.
blocks.p: What's the play: the number every ESP shows you — delivery rate — only means the mailbox provider accepted the message; it says nothing about whether it landed in the inbox or the spam folder. So a campaign can report 98% delivered while its real inbox-placement rate (IPR) is ~60% — a third of the audience never sees it, and the dashboard looks healthy. Why this is timely, not evergreen filler: through late 2025 into 2026 Gmail, Yahoo and Microsoft moved enforcement from soft-fail to permanent rejection (e.g. SPF 5.7.27, Microsoft 550 5.7.515). The bar is now hard: spam-complaint rate must stay under 0.30% (target under 0.10%), bounces under ~2%, with working one-click unsubscribe (RFC 8058). Miss it and you're blocked at the gateway, not filtered. Why it matters for our work: clients pay for revenue, and revenue tracks inbox placement — so a client whose IPR has quietly slid to 70% is losing 3-in-10 emails of pipeline while their ESP report says everything's fine. Turning that invisible leak into a measured number is a billable win and the seed of a recurring deliverability retainer.
blocks.doIt: (1) Pick one live, high-revenue campaign (a top automation or the next broadcast) and use the real final creative — not a stripped test. (2) Run a seed/spam-list test: in Litmus, Test tab → 'View and create spam tests' (or ESP Sync the draft) — it checks placement across ~11 providers (Gmail, Yahoo, Office 365, Outlook, AOL, GMX…). (3) Read folder placement per provider: Primary = pass, Promotions/Tabs = partial, Spam/Junk = reputation problem, Missing = blocked at the gateway. (4) Compute IPR = (inbox ÷ delivered) × 100; flag any major provider under ~85%. (5) Check the authentication row (SPF, DKIM, DMARC, one-click List-Unsubscribe, TLS, BIMI) — any red here is the first fix, because it's what triggers permanent rejection (card-email-dmarc-bimi). (6) Cross-check live reputation in Google Postmaster Tools (spam rate under 0.10%) and Microsoft SNDS. (7) Fix failing auth, prune low-engagement/invalid addresses, re-test until IPR clears the threshold, then ship. (8) Productize it as a one-page 'Inbox Placement Scorecard' for the client. Card: card-email-inbox-placement-audit.
blocks.note: Grounded in Litmus's deliverability guidance (the delivery-vs-placement reframing + the spam-test method) and the 2026 Gmail/Yahoo sender thresholds (0.10%/0.30%, permanent-rejection codes). The exact IPR figures are illustrative of the gap, not a promise for your list — the point is to measure yours. Fresh angle vs recent email beats (not the Omnisend MCP, not the micro-animation GIF). Logged a prediction (a major ESP surfaces IPR as a default report metric by end-Q3). Sources: Litmus (×2), Chronos Agency (2026 sender requirements).

sections.h: Paid — run it today: re-baseline your budget-limited Smart Bidding targets before Google's Aug 17 recalibration (+ two more Google Ads bidding changes)

blocks.sub: Google is changing how budget-constrained tCPA/tROAS campaigns optimize (rollout Aug 17), shipping Promotion Mode (beta) to schedule a temporary ROAS-loosen + extra budget for sales spikes, and taking Smart Bidding Exploration global — audit your limited-by-budget campaigns now
blocks.p: What changed — three Google Ads bidding/budgeting updates: (1) Bidding-target optimization for budget-limited campaigns (a backend change): Google is recalibrating how budget-constrained tCPA/tROAS campaigns optimize toward their targets so performance stays more predictable as budgets scale. Timeline (cross-checked across Search Engine Journal and Search Engine Roundtable): account notifications begin ~Jul 6, rollout starts Aug 17 and takes a few weeks, with a brief calibration window of 'minor performance fluctuations.' (2) Promotion Mode (beta) for Search + Performance Max: schedule a temporary change to ROAS tolerance plus extra daily budget around a known demand spike (launch, seasonal event, flash sale) — a native lever that replaces the manual budget/tROAS hacks teams do during sales. (3) Smart Bidding Exploration expanded globally across all languages for Search and feed-less PMax (finds incremental converting queries beyond your normal targeting without forcing you to loosen ROAS much), with a new Shopping beta for standard Shopping + PMax with feeds. Why it matters for us: update (1) will quietly move spend and CPA/ROAS on every budget-capped campaign during calibration — if a client's targets are stale or aspirational, you'll see efficiency swing and could misread a normal wobble as a bug, or let a too-loose target overspend. (3) is close to free incremental volume for accounts that feel query-starved. Get ahead of it on purpose.
blocks.doIt: (1) Find the exposed campaigns: Campaigns table → add the 'Budget' status column or filter Status: Limited by budget; flag every Search/PMax/Shopping campaign showing it. (2) For each, open Settings → Bidding and record the current Target CPA / Target ROAS, then pull trailing 30–90-day actual CPA/ROAS (Columns → add Conv., Cost/conv., Conv. value/cost). (3) Re-baseline to reality before Aug 17 — if your set target is far from trailing actuals, move it toward an achievable number so the recalibration optimizes against a sane goal. (4) Set a notifications watch (bell icon) for the bidding-target notice ~Jul 6, and screenshot it for the account log. (5) Enable Smart Bidding Exploration where you want volume (Settings → Bidding on Search / feed-less PMax); check eligibility for the Shopping beta. (6) Pre-stage Promotion Mode on your top promo campaigns now so it's ready to schedule for the next sale instead of a manual override. (7) Diary an Aug 17–Sep 7 'calibration watch': annotate accounts, hold big manual bid edits for the first ~2 weeks, judge on a rolling 14-day window, not day-over-day. Card: card-paid-google-bidding-recalibration.
blocks.note: Cross-checked: SEJ and Search Engine Roundtable agree on the Aug 17 rollout, the ~Jul 6 notifications, Promotion Mode's scheduled ROAS-tolerance + extra-budget mechanic, and the Smart Bidding Exploration global expansion + Shopping beta. Treat the Jul 6 / Aug 17 dates as the announced schedule — directional until the in-account notice lands. Secondary (worth a look, not a deadline): Microsoft Advertising launched Product Explorer — a Merchant Center tool to search/filter your catalog by feed attributes + performance, surface rejected/underperforming SKUs, and export lists (US advertisers, under 100k SKUs). Reuses the durable paid lesson: keep a guarded manual baseline to prove the AI's lift on YOUR account. Logged a prediction (Promotion Mode reaches GA for Search + PMax by end-2026). Sources: Search Engine Journal (×2), Search Engine Roundtable.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape, cross-checked: ChatGPT slips below 50% assistant share; SpaceX buys Cursor for ~$60B; SpaceX IPO total hits $85.7B
sections.intro: Markets/landscape = intake, not a craft card — but it's the climate every client conversation happens in. Every figure here is cross-checked against ≥2 independent sources; fast-moving prints are flagged directional.

blocks.sub: ChatGPT fell below 50% of AI-assistant usage for the first time — Gemini is the gainer (be precise about which metric)
blocks.p: What the data says: per Sensor Tower's end-of-May app-usage reading, ChatGPT is at 46.4% of AI-assistant usage (above 50% until January 2026), Google Gemini 27.7% (the clear gainer, riding Google-ecosystem integration), and Anthropic Claude 10.3%; Grok/Perplexity/DeepSeek/Meta AI together under 5%. The critical caveat — and the thing to get right when a client cites it: this is app/assistant usage share. A different metric, StatCounter web traffic, still puts ChatGPT near ~80% of chatbot web visits. So 'below 50%' is true and newsworthy for app usage, not a claim that ChatGPT lost the web. Why it matters for us: the assistant market is genuinely multi-model now, which is exactly why we keep building AI features for portability (a gateway with a fallback model, card-ai-tooling-model-portability) rather than hard-wiring one vendor — and why client pitches should lead with cost-per-outcome over brand allegiance.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — TechCrunch + Yahoo Finance, both attributing the Sensor Tower dataset. The 46.4/27.7/10.3 split is one tracker's app-usage estimate — directional, and explicitly NOT web-traffic share. Advances the AI-landscape thread (open-vs-closed / assistant market).

blocks.sub: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor (Anysphere) for ~$60B; SpaceX IPO total grew to $85.7B after the greenshoe
blocks.p: Two deal items extend the SpaceX arc we covered on Jun 12–13. (1) SpaceX → Cursor: SpaceX signed a definitive agreement to acquire AI-coding startup Cursor/Anysphere at ~$60B equity value, expected to close Q3 2026 — feeding xAI (which SpaceX merged with in February) a foothold in AI coding; Cursor is reportedly at ~$2.6B annualized revenue, and SpaceX exercised an April option to buy rather than partner. (2) SpaceX IPO: underwriters exercised the overallotment/greenshoe, lifting total proceeds from the Jun 12 debut to $85.7B (~83M extra shares; priced $135, closed ~$161, valuation past $2T). Why it matters for us: AI coding is consolidating into a SpaceX/xAI mega-entity alongside Anthropic and OpenAI — another reason a team's toolchain shouldn't single-point on one vendor's roadmap (keep it swap-ready). The market read stays risk-on for marquee AI/frontier bets even as chip names reprice on spend-scrutiny — a climate signal (lead with cost-per-outcome), not a trade.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Cursor deal = Reported (Reuters/Investing.com + Yahoo/Reuters + Bloomberg headline) — the ~$60B value and Q3-2026 close are confirmed, but the 'all-stock'/merger-sub structure is NOT primary-confirmed, so treat structure as reported. IPO $85.7B greenshoe = Reported (CNBC + Reuters/Investing.com); single-day prints directional. Advances the megacap-risk-on + agentic-consolidation threads.

blocks.sub: Reported sweep (2 sources each): Nvidia's $25B bond, Salesforce buys Fin for $3.6B, Threads hits 500M, SoftBank+OpenAI patching service
blocks.p: Fast scan, each cross-checked: Nvidia launched its first investment-grade bond deal since 2021, upsized to ~$25B on heavy demand (Confirmed ≥3: Bloomberg, Euronews, Reuters/NST) — raising debt instead of diluting equity as its capital commitments mount. Salesforce agreed to acquire AI customer-service platform Fin (formerly Intercom) for $3.6B, folding it into Agentforce; close expected ~early 2027 (Confirmed ≥3 incl. Salesforce's own release). Threads hit 500M monthly users and shipped a 'Your Algo' feed-control plus Communities out of beta (Meta newsroom + TechCrunch/Engadget) — a maturing channel for the social team to watch. SoftBank + OpenAI launched a 'Patching as a Service' security offering for Japan's top 3,000 critical-infrastructure firms (SoftBank release + AP). Why it matters: the through-line is AI moving from demos into balance-sheet-scale infrastructure and consolidation — the backdrop against which clients now scrutinize every AI line item.
blocks.note: Corroboration as labeled inline (Nvidia/Salesforce = Confirmed ≥3; Threads/SoftBank = Reported 2 incl. a primary newsroom). Figures cross-checked; order-book/oversubscription details on the Nvidia bond are from secondary writeups — directional. France's reported €655M AI plan is held back here: the government described the capability, not the vendor — it is NOT confirmed as Mistral-built, so we're not publishing that attribution.

sources: Vercel Changelog — Workflow SDK now supports inflight cancellation · Vercel Changelog — Functions can now run up to 30 minutes · Vercel Docs — Workflows · Litmus — Deliverability Myth: Measure Inbox Placement · Litmus — Spam Testing (inbox-placement method) · Chronos Agency — Gmail & Yahoo Sender Requirements 2026 · Search Engine Journal — Google Ads: 3 bidding updates PPC managers need to know · Search Engine Roundtable — Promotion Mode beta, Smart Bidding Exploration, bidding-target change · Search Engine Journal — Microsoft Advertising launches Product Explorer · TechCrunch — ChatGPT's market share slips below 50% · Yahoo Finance — ChatGPT share slips as Gemini gains (Sensor Tower) · Reuters/Yahoo — SpaceX to buy Cursor (Anysphere) for $60B · CNBC — SpaceX IPO total reaches $85.7B after greenshoe · TechCrunch — Salesforce acquires Fin for $3.6B · Meta Newsroom — Threads reaches 500M monthly users · SoftBank — OpenAI-powered Patching as a Service
